title: Preparation and ion conductivities of the plasticized polymer electrolytes based on the poly(acrylonitrile-co-lithium methacrylate)
abstract:
Preparation and Ion Conductivities of the Plasticized
Polymer Electrolytes Based on the Poly(acrylonitrile-
co-lithium methacrylate)
KYOUNG-HEE LEE,1 JUNG-KI PARK,1 WHI-JOONG KIM2
1Department of Chemical Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology, 373-1, Kusung-dong,
Yusung-gu, Daejon, 305-701, Korea
2Research and Development Center, HanKook Tire MFG. Co., 23-1, Jang-Dong, Yusung-gu, Daejon, 305-343, Korea
Received 30 April 1998; revised 27 July 1998; accepted 29 July 1998
ABSTRACT:
The polymer electrolytes composed of poly(acrylonitrile-co-lithium methac-
rylate) [P(AN-co-LiMA)], ethylene carbonate (EC), and LiClO4 salts have been pre-
pared. The ion groups in the P(AN-co-LiMA) were found to prevent EC from crystalli-
zation through their ion–dipole interactions with the polar groups in the EC. This
suppression of the EC crystallization could lead to the enhancement of the ion conduc-
tivity at subambient temperature. The polymer electrolytes based on the PAN ionomer
with 4 mol % ion content exhibited ion conductivities of 2.4 3 1024 S/cm at 210°C and
1.9 3 1023 S/cm at 25°C by simply using EC as a plasticizer. In the polymer electrolytes
based on the PAN ionomer, ion motions seemed to be coupled with the segmental
motions of the polymer chain due to the presence of the ion–dipole interaction between
the ion groups in the ionomer and the polar groups in the EC, while the ion transport
in the PAN-based polymer electrolytes was similar to that of the liquid electrolytes.
© 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. J Polym Sci B: Polym Phys 37: 247–252, 1999
Keywords:
poly(acrylonitrile-co-lithium methacrylate) [P(AN-co-LiMA]; ionomer;
polymer electrolyte; ion content; microscopic viscosity; ion transport mechanism
INTRODUCTION
There is considerable interest in solid polymer
electrolytes owing to their potentially wide range
of application in such areas as solid-state batter-
ies, electrochromic devices, and sensors.1–3 An
important group of materials in this respect is the
Li1- conductive solid-polymer electrolytes, com-
posed of complexes of Li salts with long chain
polymer hosts such as poly(ethylene oxide), PEO.
These conventional polymer electrolytes, how-
ever, have low room-temperature conductivities
on the order of 1028 to 1025 Scm21. These con-
ductivities are too low to satisfy the requirement
of room temperature application of the solid-state
batteries.
High ionic conductivities (.1023 Scm21) can
normally be achieved by plasticizing solid poly-
mer electrolytes with small organic molecules
such as ethylene carbonate (EC), propylene car-
bonate (PC), dimethyl carbonate (DMC), diethyl
carbonate (DEC), etc., which have high dielectric
constants and low vapor pressures. The typical
examples of such electrolytes are those based on
polyacrylonitrile (PAN),4–6 poly(vinyl chloride)
(PVC),7,8 poly(vinylidene ﬂuoride) (PVdF),9,10
poly(methyl
methacrylate)
(PMMA),11–13
etc.
Among these plasticized polymer electrolytes, the
PAN-based polymer electrolytes have been stud-
ied by many researchers due to their high room-
temperature ion conductivity and dimensional
stability.4–6 Many studies on the PAN-based
Correspondence to: J.-K. Park
Journal of Polymer Science: Part B: Polymer Physics, Vol. 37, 247–252 (1999)
© 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
CCC 0887-6266/99/030247-06
247
 polymer electrolytes have dealt with those pri-
marily containing a binary solvent mixture of EC
and PC, of which melting point and crystallinity
are depressed compared with those of EC to en-
hance
the
low-temperature
ion
conductivi-
ties.5,6,14 However, the electrolytes using PC are
reported to be not suitable for electrochemical
lithium intercalation into a graphite carbon an-
ode, which is commonly used in Li secondary bat-
tery, because graphite carbon degrades by coint-
ercalation of PC with lithium.15–17
These considerations motivated us to develop
the new high ion-conducting plasticized polymer
electrolytes based on the modiﬁed PAN in which
only EC is used as a plasticizer. In this work, the
polymer electrolytes composed of the poly(acrylo-
nitrile-co-Li methacrylate) [P(AN-co-LiMA)], EC,
and LiClO4 were prepared. The effect of the ion
group in the PAN ionomer on the ion conductivi-
ties and the ion transport mechanism in the PAN
ionomer-based polymer electrolytes were also in-
vestigated.
EXPERIMENTAL
Preparation of the P(AN-co-MAA) and Its Ionomer
The poly(acrylonitrile-co-methacrylic acid) [P(AN-
co-MAA)] copolymers were prepared by redox copo-
lymerization of acrylonitrile (AN) and methacrylic
acid (MAA) using potassium persulfate and sodium
bisulﬁte as initiators. To the reactor ﬁtted with a
mechanical stirrer, condenser, nitrogen inlet and
outlet, the mixed monomers and the distilled water
as a solvent were added, and then the initiator was
injected. The polymerization was carried out at
50°C for 6 h under nitrogen atmosphere. After the
polymerization was completed, the product was ﬁl-
tered, washed three times in the distilled water,
and dried under vacuum at 80°C. The poly(acrylo-
nitrile-co-Li methacrylate) (P(AN-co-LiMA)) iono-
mers were made by neutralizing the P(AN-co-MAA)
in dimethylformamide(DMF) with the stoichiomet-
ric amount of lithium hydroxide. The ion content of
the P(AN-co-LiMA) ionomers was controlled by the
feed composition of monomers, and was determined
from the titration method. For brevity, the P(AN-
co-LiMA) copolymers synthesized are designated as
PANx, where x indicates a mol % of LiMA unit in
the copolymer. The neutralized copolymers were
recovered by solvent casting in a hood and then
dried at 80°C under vacuum for 48 h.
Preparation of Polymer Electrolytes
The appropriate amount of the PAN ionomer, EC,
and LiClO4 were completely dissolved in DMF at
80°C. The viscous solutions were then placed on
Teﬂon plates, and the solvent was evaporated at
80°C. The thickness of the ﬁlms was 300 6 50 mm.
The composition of the resulting polymer electro-
lytes was 14.9/74.6/10.5 (polymer/EC/LiClO4) by
wt %. All the preparations were proceeded in a
glove box purged with argon. In this article, the
term “ion content” means only the relative frac-
tion of COO2Li1 unit in the PAN ionomer, not
including the salt, i.e., Li1ClO4
2.
Conductivity Measurements
Polymer electrolyte ﬁlms with the thickness 300
6 50 mm were sandwiched between the two stain-
less steel (SS) electrodes (12 mm in diameter).
The ionic conductivities of the polymer electrolyte
ﬁlms were measured by complex impedance anal-
ysis using a Solartron 1255 frequency response
analyzer coupled to an IBM PS/2 computer over a
frequency range of 100 Hz to 10 MHz. An AC
perturbation of 100 mV was applied to the cell.
The real and imaginary parts of the complex im-
pedance were plotted, and the ionic conductivity
(s) could be calculated from the bulk resistance
(Rb) found in complex impedance diagram.
Thermal Analysis
The differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) stud-
ies were carried out to determine the melting
temperatures (Tm) of the polymer electrolytes us-
ing a DuPont 9900 instrument. Samples were
loaded in hermetically sealed aluminum pans and
measurements were taken over a temperature
range of 2100 to 250°C at a heating rate of 10°C
min21 under nitrogen atmosphere.
Infrared Measurements
The samples used for FTIR studies were prepared
by casting the DMF solution of the mixture of the
P(AN-co-LiMA), EC, LiClO4 on the Krs-5 window.
FTIR spectra were recorded in the absorption
mode on a Bomem MB 102 FTIR spectrometer
covering a range from 400 to 4000 cm21. The
resolution for all spectra was 4 cm21.
RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
Characterization of Ionomer
By comparing the FTIR spectra for the unneutral-
ized copolymer of AN and MAA, and the corre-

Preparation of the P(AN-co-MAA) and Its Ionomer
The poly(acrylonitrile-co-methacrylic acid) [P(AN-
co-MAA)] copolymers were prepared by redox copo-
lymerization of acrylonitrile (AN) and methacrylic
acid (MAA) using potassium persulfate and sodium
bisulﬁte as initiators. To the reactor ﬁtted with a
mechanical stirrer, condenser, nitrogen inlet and
outlet, the mixed monomers and the distilled water
as a solvent were added, and then the initiator was
injected. The polymerization was carried out at
50°C for 6 h under nitrogen atmosphere. After the
polymerization was completed, the product was ﬁl-
tered, washed three times in the distilled water,
and dried under vacuum at 80°C. The poly(acrylo-
nitrile-co-Li methacrylate) (P(AN-co-LiMA)) iono-
mers were made by neutralizing the P(AN-co-MAA)
in dimethylformamide(DMF) with the stoichiomet-
ric amount of lithium hydroxide. The ion content of
the P(AN-co-LiMA) ionomers was controlled by the
feed composition of monomers, and was determined
from the titration method. For brevity, the P(AN-
co-LiMA) copolymers synthesized are designated as
PANx, where x indicates a mol % of LiMA unit in
the copolymer. The neutralized copolymers were
recovered by solvent casting in a hood and then
dried at 80°C under vacuum for 48 h.
Preparation of Polymer Electrolytes
The appropriate amount of the PAN ionomer, EC,
and LiClO4 were completely dissolved in DMF at
80°C. The viscous solutions were then placed on
Teﬂon plates, and the solvent was evaporated at
80°C. The thickness of the ﬁlms was 300 6 50 mm.
The composition of the resulting polymer electro-
lytes was 14.9/74.6/10.5 (polymer/EC/LiClO4) by
wt %. All the preparations were proceeded in a
glove box purged with argon. In this article, the
term “ion content” means only the relative frac-
tion of COO2Li1 unit in the PAN ionomer, not
including the salt, i.e., Li1ClO4
Polymer electrolyte ﬁlms with the thickness 300
6 50 mm were sandwiched between the two stain-
less steel (SS) electrodes (12 mm in diameter).
The ionic conductivities of the polymer electrolyte
ﬁlms were measured by complex impedance anal-
ysis using a Solartron 1255 frequency response
analyzer coupled to an IBM PS/2 computer over a
frequency range of 100 Hz to 10 MHz. An AC
perturbation of 100 mV was applied to the cell.
The real and imaginary parts of the complex im-
pedance were plotted, and the ionic conductivity
(s) could be calculated from the bulk resistance
(Rb) found in complex impedance diagram.
The differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) stud-
ies were carried out to determine the melting
temperatures (Tm) of the polymer electrolytes us-
ing a DuPont 9900 instrument. Samples were
loaded in hermetically sealed aluminum pans and
measurements were taken over a temperature
range of 2100 to 250°C at a heating rate of 10°C
min21 under nitrogen atmosphere.
The samples used for FTIR studies were prepared
by casting the DMF solution of the mixture of the
P(AN-co-LiMA), EC, LiClO4 on the Krs-5 window.
FTIR spectra were recorded in the absorption
mode on a Bomem MB 102 FTIR spectrometer
covering a range from 400 to 4000 cm21. The
resolution for all spectra was 4 cm21.
By comparing the FTIR spectra for the unneutral-
ized copolymer of AN and MAA, and the corre-
sponding Li ionomer, it is possible to ascertain the
chemical structure of the ionomer. In Figure 1,
the expanded FTIR spectra for the unneutralized
copolymer and its Li ionomer are shown. For the
unneutralized copolymer, the peak for the C5O
stretching of the carboxylic acid group in MAA
unit was observed in the region of 1690–1770
cm21. On the other hand, this peak disappeared
and a new peak in the region of 1550–1650 cm21
appeared for the ionomer. This peak corresponds
to the asymmetric stretching vibration of the car-
boxylate ion (COO2) that is bonded to the metal
ion,18,19 which conﬁrms the formation of the iono-
mer. The peak in the region of 1650–1690 cm21 is
corresponding to the C5O stretching of the resid-
Ion Group Effect on the Ion Conductivities
The ion conductivities of the polymer electrolytes
as a function of ion content in the ionomer are
shown in Figure 2. It is found that the ion con-
ductivities of the polymer electrolytes increase,
reach a maximum, and then decrease as the ion
content in the PAN ionomer increases. The initial
increase of the ion conductivities with the ion
content can be expected for the following two rea-
sons. The ﬁrst reason is related to the fact that
the ion groups in the ionomer prevent EC from
crystallization due to the good miscibility be-
tween the ion groups in the ionomer and EC. In
Figure 3, the DSC thermograms for the polymer
electrolytes with different ion content are repre-
sented. As seen from the Figure 3, the melting
endothermic peak around 15°C, which is corre-
sponding to the melting transition of the EC crys-
tal, is observed for the polymer electrolyte based
on PAN. This endothermic peak becomes weaker
for the polymer electrolyte based on the PAN4
ionomer, and ﬁnally disappears for the polymer
electrolytes based on the PAN7 and the PAN11
ionomers. These results mean that the ion groups
in the ionomer hinder the crystallization of EC.
This suppression of the EC crystallization is ex-
pected to result from the strong ion–dipole inter-
action between the ion groups in the ionomer and
the polar groups of EC. The presence of the ion–
dipole interaction between the ion groups in the
ionomer and the polar groups of the molecule was
already reported by many other workers.18–20 Be-
cause the crystalline phase of EC cannot trans-
port the charge carrier and hinder the motion of
the charge carrier, the disruption of the EC crys-
tal is expected to be a main factor for the initial
FTIR spectra of the unneutralized copoly-
mer of AN and MAA, and the P(AN-co-LiMA).
Ion conductivities of the polymer electro-
lytes as a function of ion content in the ionomer.
DSC thermograms of the polymer electro-
lytes as a function of ion content in the ionomer.
PLASTICIZED POLYMER ELECTROLYTES
increase in the ion conductivities with the in-
crease of the ion content. The other reason for the
initial increase of the ion conductivities with the
ion content is associated with the increment of the
charge carrier by the increase of the ion content.
Because the lithium cations from the ionomer as
well as the lithium cations from lithium perchlor-
ate can also contribute to the ion conduction, the
total concentration of the charge carrier should
increase with the increase of the ion content.
However, the ﬁrst reason, i.e., the suppression of
the EC crystallization, will mainly contribute to
amount of the lithium cation from the ionomer is
quite small compared with that of the added salt,
which provides most of the charge carrier in the
In Figure 2, it is also found that the initial
increase of the ion conductivities with the ion
content at the temperatures above 15°C, is much
less signiﬁcant than that below 15°C. This result
is attributed to the fact that the ion groups in the
ionomer contribute to only the increase in the
number of charge carrier because there are no EC
However, the increase of the ion conductivities
by the disruption of the EC crystal and the incre-
ment of the charge carrier is offset by the de-
crease in the mobility of the charge carrier at the
higher ion content. The reason for the decrease in
the mobility of the charge carrier with increase of
the ion content is related to the fact that the
ion–dipole interaction between the matrix poly-
mer(ion groups in the ionomer) and the plasticiz-
er(the polar groups in the EC) increases the mi-
croscopic viscosity of the EC phase. According to
other works,4,21,22 the PAN-based polymer elec-
trolytes have the phase-separated structure that
consists of the polymer-rich phase and the plasti-
cizer-rich phase, and charge carriers are trans-
ported mainly through the plasticizer-rich phase,
which is mainly composed of the plasticizer and
the salt. In that case, the microscopic viscosity of
the plasticizer-rich phase governs the mobility of
the charge carriers, and consequently, low micro-
scopic viscosity is required for obtaining the high
ion conductivity. In the case of the PAN ionomer-
based polymer electrolytes, the microscopic vis-
cosity of the plasticizer-rich phase will be more
signiﬁcantly increased than in the case of the
PAN-based polymer electrolytes, due to the ion–
dipole interactions between the plasticizer and
the ion groups in the ionomer. In our PAN iono-
mer-based polymer electrolyte systems, the ion
groups in the ionomers will interact with the plas-
ticizer, i.e., EC. These interactions increase the
introduction of the polymer unit that is bonded
with the ion groups into the plasticizer-rich
phase, and consequently increase the microscopic
viscosity of the plasticizer phase. The decrease in
the charge carrier mobility due to the increase of
the microscopic viscosity is expected to be the
reason for the decrease of the ion conductivity at
the higher ion content. This conclusion is also
supported by the electric modulus studies for the
polymer electrolytes based on PAN and the PAN
ionomer. Figure 4 shows a plot of the imaginary
part of the electric modulus vs. frequency for the
polymer electrolytes with different ion content. It
is well known that the peak shown in the plot of
the imaginary part of the electric modulus vs.
frequency is related to the conductivity relax-
ation. A relaxation process can be characterized
by a time constant, t 5 1/(2pfc), where fc is a peak
frequency in the plot of the imaginary part of the
electric modulus vs. frequency, and the relaxation
time is related to the mobility of the charge car-
riers: the longer t, the slower ionic motion.23,24 In
Figure 5, the relaxation times calculated from the
peak frequency shown in Figure 4 are presented
as a function of ion content in the ionomer. It is
found that the relaxation time increases with in-
creasing the ion content, indicating that the mo-
bility of the charge carrier decreases as the ion
As known from the previous work,25 the de-
crease in the number of the charge carrier from
the added salts, lithium perchlorate, with in-
crease in the ion content in the ionomer can be
also a reason for the decrease in the ion conduc-
Imaginary part of the electric modulus vs.
log frequency as a function of ion content in the iono-
tivity at the higher ion content, but this effect is
thought to be negligible because the number of
the charge carrier does not decrease signiﬁcantly
as the ion content in the ionomer increases at the
higher plasticizer content (.400 wt %).
Ion Transport Mechanism of PAN and the PAN
Ionomer-Based Polymer Electrolytes
To consider the mechanism of ion transport for
the polymer electrolytes prepared from PAN and
the PAN ionomers, the temperature dependency
of the ion conductivity was investigated. Figure 6
shows the Arrhenius plot of the ion conductivities
for the polymer electrolytes based on PAN and the
PAN ionomers. In the case of the polymer electro-
lyte based on PAN, the discrete change of the ion
conductivities with the reciprocal temperature
was observed, which could be explained with ther-
mal analysis of the polymer electrolytes. As seen
from the DSC results shown in Figure 3, the
melting transition of the EC crystal appears
around 15°C. Because the crystalline phase of EC
cannot transport the charge carrier, the melting
of the EC crystal results in the discrete increase
in the ion conductivities around the melting tem-
perature. This discrete increase in the ion conduc-
tivities was also observed in the polymer electro-
lyte based on the PAN4 ionomer, but it is less
prominent compared with the PAN-based poly-
mer electrolytes, due to the small amount of the
EC crystal present in the polymer electrolytes as
shown from the DSC results in Figure 3. In the
polymer electrolytes based on the PAN11 iono-
mer, the discrete increase of the ion conductivity
was not observed due to the absence of the EC
crystal in the polymer electrolytes, and the tem-
perature dependency of the ion conductivities
It is well known that in the case of the PAN-
based polymer electrolytes, the conductivity pro-
ﬁle with the reciprocal temperature looks similar
to that of the liquid electrolytes, and that the
value of Arrhenius activation energy of this poly-
mer electrolyte is close to those of the liquid elec-
trolytes.4,21,26 As mentioned above, this means
that the ion conducting path in the PAN-based
polymer electrolytes is the plasticizer-rich phase
which is mainly composed of plasticizers such as
EC, PC, DMC, DEC, etc., and the salts, and that
the effect of the polymer matrix on the ion trans-
port is not signiﬁcant. In the case of the PAN
ionomer systems, there is ion–dipole interactions
between the ion groups in the ionomer and the
oxygens in the EC in the polymer electrolytes.
These ion–dipole interactions increase the num-
ber of polymer chains incorporated into the plas-
ticizer-rich phase (it means that the miscibility
between the polymer matrix and the plasticizer
increases and, thus, polymer electrolytes becomes
more homogeneous as the ion content in the iono-
mer increases), and consequently, the polymer
chains take part in the transport of the charge
carrier. Therefore, in the polymer electrolytes
based on the PAN ionomers, the free volume gen-
eration of the polymer chain in the plasticizer-
rich phase becomes also important for the ion
transport. The free-volume generation of the poly-
mer chain in the plasticizer-rich phase becomes
Temperature dependence of ion conductiv-
ities for the polymer electrolytes based on the PAN,
Relaxation time for the ion conductivity
relaxation as a function of ion content in the ionomer
PLASTICIZED POLYMER ELECTROLYTES
more signiﬁcant as the ion content in the ionomer
increases. This conclusion is supported by the
inspection of the activation energy for the PAN
and the PAN ionomer-based electrolytes. Table I
shows the activation energies for conduction cal-
culated from the slope of the common logarithm of
the ion conductivity (log s) vs. the reciprocal tem-
perature (1/T) plot. Because it was intended to
explain the effect of polymer chain on the ion
conduction mechanism for the case of the PAN
and the PAN ionomer-based polymer electrolytes,
it was necessary to exclude the effect of EC crystal
on the ion conduction mechanism. Therefore, the
four points above EC melting temperature, 15°C,
were selected for calculating the activation en-
ergy in the case of the PAN and PAN4-based
polymer electrolytes. In the case of the PAN11-
based polymer electrolytes, however, there are no
EC crystals. Therefore, it is thought to be more
reasonable to calculate the activation energy from
all the points for the PAN11-based polymer elec-
trolyte. It is noted that the activation energies of
the PAN4 and the PAN11-based polymer electro-
lytes are larger than that of the PAN-based poly-
mer electrolyte. Considering that the conven-
tional polymer electrolytes of which ion transport
mechanism can be described by the free-volume
theory have larger activation energies than those
of the liquid electrolytes,4,21,26 the larger values of
the activation energy are indicative of the fact
that the ionic motions are coupled with the seg-
mental motions of the polymer chain for the poly-
mer electrolytes based on the PAN ionomer.
We are now conducting a study on the effect of
the ion groups in the ionomer on the stability of
the Li/PAN ionomer-based polymer electrolytes
interface. The interface of the Li/PAN ionomer-
based polymer electrolytes was found to be more
stable than that of the Li/PAN-based polymer
electrolytes. More detailed studies will be re-
